title.emphasis: Part II: The California Paradox: Obscurity in Law, Clarity in Consent
2.1 The Logic of Obscurity: Navigating Penal Code § 647(b) and the Legacy of

section.para: The central conflict under examination is born from two mutually exclusive legal and communicative paradigms operating simultaneously in California. The first is a logic of obscurity, a set of defensive maneuvers compelled by the state's criminalization of prostitution. California Penal Code § 647(b) makes it a crime to solicit, agree to engage in, or engage in a "lewd act" for "money or other consideration".2 A "lewd act" is defined as the touching of intimate parts for the purpose of sexual arousal or gratification.3 Critically, the statute targets not just the act but the

agreement itself, requiring only an "overt act" in furtherance of the agreement for a crime to be complete.3 This focus on the bargain transforms any explicit negotiation linking sex and value into a criminal act.

In response to this legal peril, individuals in relationships with a significant financial component have been advised to adopt a series of "workarounds" designed to obscure the nature of their agreement rather than affirm its legitimacy.7 These strategies are predicated on an assumption of illegality and are fundamentally defensive. The first strategy is to frame financial support as a "gift." Under California Civil Code § 1146, a gift is a voluntary transfer made without consideration.8 By characterizing payments as gifts, parties attempt to sever the

quid pro quo element required for a prostitution charge, re-characterizing the exchange to negate the appearance of a bargain.8 The second strategy is to "decouple" conversations about finances and intimacy.9 By creating a temporal and causal gap between discussions of money and discussions of sex, it becomes more difficult for a prosecutor to prove that one was the explicit consideration for the other.
The third and most legally sophisticated strategy relies on the doctrine of "severable, non-sexual consideration" established in the landmark case Marvin v. Marvin.8 The

Marvin court held that unmarried cohabitants could enforce express or implied contracts for property and support, but only to the extent that the agreement did not rest upon "the immoral and illicit consideration of meretricious sexual services".11 This created a path for enforcement if the financial support could be tied to lawful services like homemaking, companionship, and emotional support, while the sexual component of the relationship was legally ignored.8

The Marvin doctrine, while celebrated for providing remedies to unmarried partners, created a form of institutionalized hypocrisy. It allows courts to enforce the economic reality of an integrated, intimate relationship while simultaneously requiring them to legally pretend that the sexual component of that same relationship is not part of the bargain. In the lived reality of a long-term partnership, services like companionship and homemaking are inextricably intertwined with the sexual relationship; they are part of a single relational package.8 When a court enforces a

Marvin agreement by valuing the "severable" services, it is knowingly engaging in a legal fiction. It is valuing the entire relationship but using the non-sexual services as the legal justification to circumvent the public policy against prostitution. This judicial maneuver is the cornerstone of the logic of obscurity, a state-sanctioned method of misrepresenting an agreement to achieve an equitable result.

title.emphasis: 2.2 The Mandate of Clarity: The Non-Negotiable Pillars of Affirmative Consent

section.para: In stark opposition to the logic of obscurity stands California's mandate for absolute clarity in sexual consent. The state's "Yes Means Yes" standard, which has profoundly influenced legal and social norms, is an exacting framework designed to eliminate ambiguity, not create it.13 This standard is built on several non-negotiable pillars that are fundamentally incompatible with the defensive workarounds discussed above.

First, consent must be Active and Affirmative. California law defines consent as "positive cooperation in act or attitude pursuant to an exercise of free will".17 It must be expressed through clear words or actions that indicate a willingness to participate. The law is unequivocal that silence, passivity, or the absence of resistance does not constitute consent.13

Second, consent must be Ongoing and Revocable. It is not a one-time agreement but a continuous process that must be present throughout a sexual encounter.9 Most critically, consent can be revoked at any point, for any reason. Once consent is withdrawn, the sexual activity must cease immediately.14 This principle of radical revocability fundamentally repositions sexual consent outside the realm of traditional contract law. A contract is an agreement that, once formed, creates a binding obligation, and unilateral withdrawal constitutes a breach with potential consequences.17 Affirmative consent, in contrast, is a state of being that creates no future obligation and can be unilaterally terminated without penalty. The right of revocation overrides any prior promise, making the two concepts legally and philosophically incompatible.

Third, consent must be Specific. Consent to one form of sexual activity, such as kissing, does not imply consent to other forms, such as intercourse.13 Similarly, a past dating relationship or prior sexual history is never, by itself, an indicator of consent for a current encounter.13

Fourth, the legal domain of affirmative consent is the Physical Act, not the Bargain. Its purpose is to protect bodily autonomy and prevent assault.13 It is concerned with a person's voluntary willingness to engage in a physical act of touching, entirely separate from any financial or contractual arrangements that may exist between the parties.

title.emphasis: 2.3 The Irreconcilable Conflict: Why Defensive Workarounds Cannot Ground Affirmative Consent

section.para: The strategies designed to navigate prostitution laws cannot form the basis for affirmative consent because they operate on a different legal and ethical plane and are driven by a contradictory logic. The two legal regimes impose mutually exclusive communicative duties on individuals, creating a legal Catch-22 where full compliance with the law is logically impossible in relationships that blend intimacy and finance.

The Focus on the Bargain versus the Focus on the Body highlights the different domains these laws regulate. The workarounds are entirely concerned with the legality of the exchange—the contract—to ensure a financial arrangement is not classified as prostitution. Affirmative consent is entirely concerned with the voluntariness of the physical act—the touching—to ensure a person's body is not violated. A legally "safe" financial arrangement says nothing about whether physical consent was freely and enthusiastically given in the moment.

The Logic of Obscurity versus the Logic of Clarity reveals the opposing communicative mandates. The workarounds are fundamentally about creating ambiguity to avoid legal risk. They thrive on the idea that the "real" reason for the financial exchange is unclear. Affirmative consent demands the exact opposite: absolute clarity. It requires an unambiguous, communicated "yes" to ensure physical and emotional safety. One cannot obscure their way into affirmative consent.

Finally, the conflict between Past Agreement and Present Willingness underscores the temporal disconnect. The workarounds are about structuring the ex ante agreement—the setup of the relationship. Affirmative consent is about the real-time, in-the-moment reality of a physical encounter. A person could have a perfectly legal Marvin agreement based on "companionship" and still be the victim of sexual assault if their partner ignores their revocation of consent. The legality of their cohabitation contract offers no defense against a lack of ongoing, revocable consent.

The state, through its laws, is simultaneously commanding citizens to be ambiguous and to be clear in their intimate and financial negotiations. To comply with PC § 647(b), individuals are advised to obscure the link between money and sex.7 To comply with affirmative consent law, they are required to obtain clear, unambiguous agreement for sexual acts.14 In a relationship involving both, a person cannot perform both of these legally mandated communicative acts. To be clear about the terms of the relationship is to risk a prostitution charge; to be ambiguous is to risk a sexual assault charge. This is not just a conflict; it is an impossible legal bind created by the state itself.
